main_summary.summary: Simeon Wright, cousin of Emmett Till and eyewitness to the August 1955 kidnapping in Money, Mississippi, recorded by Joe Mosnier in Argo, Illinois on May 23, 2011 for the Smithsonian and Library of Congress Civil Rights Oral History project. Wright, twelve years old at the time, describes his parents Moses and Elizabeth Wright, the arrival of Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. on the family's Mississippi farm, the Wednesday August 24, 1955 visit to Bryant's Store, Till's whistle at Carolyn Bryant, and the early-morning kidnapping by Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam. He recounts identifying Emmett's ring as a material witness, sitting in the witness room during the Sumner trial, and his father's testimony naming Milam in court. Wright describes the family's relocation to Argo, Illinois after the not-guilty verdict, his religious conversion at age twenty-four in 1967, his pipefitter career at Reynolds Metals, the 2004-2005 federal reopening of the case with Keith Beauchamp and Alvin Sykes, the exhumation and DNA identification at Burr Oak Cemetery, the Till Bill legislation, and his decision to write Simeon's Story to correct what he calls the inaccuracies in published accounts.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Wright is one of the last surviving direct eyewitnesses to the events at Bryant's Store and the kidnapping from the Wright family home in Money, Mississippi. He shared a bed with Emmett Till the night of the abduction and was present for both the store encounter and Milam and Bryant entering the bedroom. His testimony directly contradicts several widely circulated accounts of the case, including claims that the cousins dared Till to enter the store, that Till had photographs of white girlfriends, that Till touched Carolyn Bryant inside the store, that Maurice Wright accepted fifty cents store credit to help locate the house, and James Hicks's account that Moses Wright escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

The interview also documents the 2004-2005 federal reopening of the case: Wright's meeting with U.S. Attorney Greenleaf, the role of filmmaker Keith Beauchamp and Alvin Sykes, the exhumation at Burr Oak Cemetery in Chicago where Wright served as the DNA donor, prosecutor Joyce Childs's grand-jury proceeding against Carolyn Bryant, and the passage of the Till Bill funding federal investigation of civil-rights-era cold cases. Wright's first-person account of his father's decision to testify against Milam in open court, supported by Medgar Evers and the NAACP, establishes Moses Wright's testimony as the documented act it was rather than the embellished version in some published sources.

chapters.title: Parents and Mississippi Origins
chapters.summary: Wright describes his father Moses, a hard but fair Mississippi farmer who refused to leave the state, and his mother Elizabeth, raised in Hazlehurst and educated enough to teach school in Memphis. The parents met at the Churches of God in Christ headquarters in Memphis and married in Sumner, Mississippi in 1925, where Elizabeth's family already lived near what would later become the Emmett Till trial venue.

chapters.title: Writing Simeon's Story
chapters.summary: Wright explains that watching documentaries containing what he describes as inaccurate accounts of the Till case led his wife to suggest he write his own book. He identifies specific recurring inaccuracies he sought to correct, including the claim that the cousins dared Till to speak to Carolyn Bryant and the account that Moses Wright escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

chapters.title: Emmett's Arrival in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Wright recounts the anticipation of cousin Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. arriving from Chicago in late August 1955, just before cotton harvest. He describes Till as a storyteller who told them about Lincoln Park and Riverview Park in Chicago. They shared a bedroom and a bed; Till tried picking cotton on Monday but could not tolerate the heat.

chapters.title: Bryant's Store Wednesday Night
chapters.summary: On Wednesday August 24, 1955, after a full day of cotton picking, six boys drove to Money. Wheeler Parker Jr. and then Till entered Bryant's Store; Maurice sent Simeon in behind Till. Wright states Till completed his purchase without incident, but as Carolyn Bryant walked to her car outside, Till whistled at her. The boys ran to the car and left town within minutes.

chapters.title: The Kidnapping
chapters.summary: After returning from Greenwood late Saturday night, Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam came to the Wright house around 2 a.m. on Sunday August 28, 1955. They first entered Wheeler Parker's bedroom, declared him the wrong boy, then took Till from the bed he shared with Wright. Milam ordered Wright back to sleep, threatened Moses Wright with death if he reported it, and a woman's voice outside confirmed Till as the right one before they drove away.

chapters.title: Body Found and Trial
chapters.summary: Till's body was found in the Tallahatchie River about twenty miles north of the Wright home. Leflore County Sheriff George Smith intervened to stop a hasty burial. Wright identified Till's ring as a material witness in the witness room at the Sumner trial. He describes Sheriff Strider's later testimony for the defense regarding the body's identification.

chapters.title: Moses Wright's Testimony and Move to Chicago
chapters.summary: Moses Wright testified against Milam and Bryant despite neighbors warning he would be killed, with Medgar Evers and the NAACP providing protection. After the not-guilty verdict, Moses told the family they had to leave Mississippi. The family relocated to Argo Summit, Illinois, where the elder Wright could only find restaurant cleanup work; older brother James provided rent-free housing in his four-flat.

chapters.title: King in Chicago and Religious Conversion
chapters.summary: Wright initially rejected Martin Luther King's nonviolent message during the 1966 Chicago campaign at Marquette Park, unwilling to refuse to retaliate against violence. At age twenty-four in February 1967, sitting in a tavern, Wright heard an inner voice and within two weeks quit drinking, smoking, and committed his life to Christ, after which he embraced nonviolence.

chapters.title: Working Life and Reverend Parker
chapters.summary: Wright describes his work history from shining shoes to bowling-alley pinsetter to Dearborn Glass to Reynolds Metals, where he completed the Local 597 pipefitting apprenticeship in 1968 and worked until retiring in 1994. He describes Wheeler Parker Jr.'s influence drawing him toward Christ and notes Parker is now his pastor while Wright serves as deacon and teaches second and third grade Sunday school.

chapters.title: Federal Reopening 2004-2005
chapters.summary: Filmmaker Keith Beauchamp persuaded Wright to talk on camera, then collaborated with Alvin Sykes to find federal violations justifying reopening. U.S. Attorney Greenleaf in Mississippi promised reinvestigation. Till's body was exhumed at Burr Oak Cemetery, with Wright as the DNA donor; the testing confirmed identity and refuted claims of castration and missing teeth. Prosecutor Joyce Childs ultimately declined to indict Carolyn Bryant, citing insufficient evidence despite a witness placing her in the truck.

chapters.title: Till Bill and Correcting the Record
chapters.summary: Wright describes traveling to Washington with his wife to watch debate on the Till Bill, federal legislation that set aside funding for civil-rights-era cold cases. He critiques James Hicks's account of his father saying "Thar he" in court (Wright cites the trial transcript reading "There he is") and Hicks's coffin-escape story. He dismisses William Bradford Huie's Look magazine piece, including the claim Huie interviewed the family in Argo and the fifty-cents-store-credit story about Maurice Wright in Emerge Magazine.

chapters.title: Forgiveness, Legacy, Closing
chapters.summary: Wright explains forgiveness of Bryant and Milam as a religious imperative that does not preclude justice but releases him from vengeance. He compares broken hearts to scar tissue, citing Jeremiah 18 on the potter's house. He assesses the case's legacy: federal law has changed, hearts have not; racism is less overt than under Jim Crow but persists. He closes with advice to young people: go to school, respect elders, do the time if you do the crime.
